transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you for calling Sunrise Recovery Center, this is Shirley. How can I help?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Hi, I'm Catherine. I'm just... calling to find out what you guys do. I don't know if I really need treatment or whatever. My sister kind of pushed me to call.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I'm glad your sister encouraged you to reach out, and I'm glad you followed through. There's no pressure here — I'm just going to ask a few questions and we can figure out together whether what you're dealing with is something we can help with. What's been going on?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I drink, but it's not like I'm an alcoholic or anything. I just drink after work to unwind. Everyone does that.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I hear you. Can I ask — how many drinks is 'unwinding' for you on a typical evening?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: I don't know. Three or four, maybe.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: And are there nights when it's more than that?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Sometimes. If it was a really bad day, maybe six or seven.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: And how often are the really bad days?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: More than they used to be. Maybe three or four times a week now.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Okay. So on those nights — after six or seven drinks — how do you feel the next morning?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Rough. I've been calling in late to work a few times.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Has that happened more in the last few months than it used to?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yeah. My sister noticed that too. She said I've changed.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I appreciate you sharing that. Catherine, I want to reflect something back to you — not to label it, just so you can hear it. What you're describing — four to seven drinks most evenings, increasingly difficult mornings, calling in late, your sister noticing a change — those are patterns that tend to escalate without support. You don't have to call it anything. But the question isn't whether it's 'bad enough.' The question is whether it's getting in the way of the life you want.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: When you put it like that... yeah, it is.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Then you called the right place. Let me ask a few more questions. Do you have insurance?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yes. Meridian Care. Member ID is BP62919803, group GRP-560846.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you. And have you ever tried to cut back on your own?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: A few times. I go a week and then something comes up.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: That pattern — trying and returning — is really common and it's one of the things treatment specifically addresses. Here's what I'd like to do: I'll have our benefits team verify your coverage and an admissions coordinator call you back within two hours. They'll talk through your options — which based on what you've described would likely start with our outpatient track. Would that work for you?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yeah. Okay. That sounds okay.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Good. You'll hear from us by noon. And Catherine — calling was the right thing to do.

gold_labels.coaching_notes: Exemplary motivational approach — agent used Socratic questioning to let the caller arrive at their own concern rather than confronting the minimisation directly. The reflection ('the question is whether it's getting in the way of the life you want') is textbook MI and avoids labelling. Clinical screen strong: drew out frequency, morning consequences, and prior quit attempts. Objection handling 5: handled 'I don't really need this' without pushback.
